reworded.name: Information Sequencing
reworded.old: The core message is delivered early — the lede is never buried. Emotional acknowledgment of impact lands before the difficult news, not after it as a footnote. Information flows in the order readers need to cognitively and emotionally process it.
reworded.new: The email follows a strict arc: acknowledge the situation or effort first (one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters, then one concrete next step. Violating this order — even if all elements are present — breaks the flow. This is non-negotiable.

reworded.name: Audience-Centric Framing
reworded.old: Impact is framed in terms of how it affects the reader's work, plans, and experience — not the organization's reputation or management's concerns. The reader is the subject of 'why this matters' reasoning.
reworded.new: Impact is framed in terms of how it affects the reader's work, plans, and experience — not the organization's reputation or management's concerns. The reader is the subject of 'why this matters' reasoning. Drafts that center the sender's logic feel tone-deaf.

reworded.name: Actionable Specificity
reworded.old: Every ask has a named owner and a timeframe. No vague group instructions that nobody owns. If someone reads an action item, they know exactly who does what by when.
reworded.new: Every ask has one named owner and a timeframe. No group instructions that nobody owns. If multiple people need to do different things, the email designates a single point of accountability — but doesn't over-specify org structure details that the email has no business assuming.

reworded.name: Tonal Discipline
reworded.old: No motivational-poster language or sentimental closings. Emotional beats never appear where the reader is ready to act. The email ends on momentum — a clean handoff, a next step, or a forward-looking line — not a pat on the back.
reworded.new: No motivational-poster language, sentimental closings, or trailing sentiment after the CTA. The email ends on momentum — a clean handoff or forward-looking line. Anything after the action item weakens it.

reworded.name: Directness and Transparency
reworded.old: Bad news is delivered clearly and promptly, not softened into ambiguity. Reasoning is stated openly. The tone is professional but human — no panic signals, no clinical detachment.
reworded.new: Bad news is delivered clearly and promptly, not softened into ambiguity. Reasoning is stated openly. The tone is professional but human — no panic signals, no corporate detachment, no jargon.

reworded.name: Scannability
reworded.old: Key information is broken into structured, scannable formats — bullets, bold labels, clear sections. A busy reader can extract takeaways in a single pass without reading every line.
reworded.new: Short paragraphs. Key information is easy to extract in a single pass. A busy reader doesn't need to read every line to get the point and know what to do.

dimensions_changed.name: Information Sequencing
added_dims: The core message or decision is delivered after the acknowledgment, not buried further down
One concrete next step closes the body — the arc ends on action, not explanation
The 'why it matters' explanation follows the news — not before it, not after the CTA
All four elements appear in sequence: acknowledge → news → stakes → next step
Opens with a brief (one sentence max) acknowledgment of the situation or effort — not the news
removed_dims: Acknowledgment of impact on the reader precedes the difficult information
Information is ordered to match how readers process the situation (context → news → implications → action)
The core message or decision is delivered early and clearly
The rationale is delivered close to the decision, not separated from it

dimensions_changed.name: Actionable Specificity
added_dims: Action items don't assume org structure details or internal processes the email shouldn't contain
Action items identify a specific person or role responsible — not a group
Each ask describes a specific, doable action — not a vague posture like 'stay tuned' or 'please review'
removed_dims: Each ask describes a specific, doable action — not a vague posture like 'stand by'
Action items identify a specific person or role responsible

dimensions_changed.name: Tonal Discipline
added_dims: No trailing sentiment, thanks-for-your-patience lines, or filler after the call to action
removed_dims: Emotional language does not appear at points where the reader is ready to act or move on

dimensions_changed.name: Directness and Transparency
added_dims: Free of corporate jargon, buzzwords, and filler phrases
removed_dims: Language does not signal uncertainty or panic through excessive hedging or apology

dimensions_changed.name: Scannability
added_dims: Each paragraph has a single clear purpose and doesn't mix concerns
Multi-part information uses bullets, headers, or numbered lists where appropriate
Paragraphs are short — no walls of text
removed_dims: Multi-part information uses bullets, headers, or numbered lists
Each section or paragraph has a single clear purpose and doesn't mix concerns
Important details are visually distinct through formatting (bold, bullets, etc.)

reasoning: I made three main changes: First, I elevated Information Sequencing to priority 1 and rewrote it to enforce the strict four-part arc (acknowledge → news → stakes → action) as a non-negotiable order, not just a loose preference. Second, I updated Actionable Specificity to explicitly call out both failure modes — group asks with no owner AND over-specified action items that assume org structure — since the rubric only caught the first one. Third, in Tonal Discipline I replaced the vague 'momentum over sentiment' dimension with a sharper 'nothing after CTA' rule, and added a no-jargon dimension to Directness since that's a dealbreaker that wasn't explicitly represented anywhere.

reviewed_changes.original_text: That date drives your planning from here. If you're managing dependencies, resourcing decisions, or external commitments tied to this launch, the milestones below are the sequence you're working against.
reviewed_changes.new_text: That date drives your planning from here — if you're managing dependencies, resourcing decisions, or external commitments tied to this launch, the milestones below are the sequence you're working against.
reviewed_changes.reason: Information Sequencing (description updated to mandate strict arc; 'why_third' and 'action_last' dimensions added): merged the two-sentence 'why it matters' paragraph into one sentence with an em-dash to tighten the arc between news and milestones, keeping the explanation close to the decision before action items follow — satisfying the stricter sequencing order without a paragraph break that softens momentum
Directness and Transparency (new dimension 'no_jargon': free of corporate jargon, buzzwords, and filler phrases): 'surface them to' is corporate jargon; 'flag them to' is plain, direct language

reviewed_changes.original_text:
The next planning cycle kicks off [DATE]. [NAME/ROLE] will send the kickoff brief by [DATE].

Questions before then go to [NAME/EMAIL].
reviewed_changes.new_text: To get ready for kickoff: [NAME/ROLE] will send the brief by [DATE]. Send any questions to [NAME/EMAIL] before then.
reviewed_changes.reason: Tonal Discipline (new dimension 'nothing_after_cta': no trailing sentiment or filler after the call to action) and Information Sequencing (new dimension 'action_last': one concrete next step closes the body): the original split the closing into two separate paragraphs — a standalone 'Questions go to' line after the action step created trailing content after the CTA. Consolidated into one closing sentence that ends on a single action, with contact info embedded rather than orphaned. Also removed 'planning cycle' as filler jargon per Directness and Transparency 'no_jargon' dimension.
reviewed_changes.user_feedback: The intent is right — I don't want trailing content after the CTA — but 'To get ready for kickoff' feels like filler framing. Just lead with the action directly: '[NAME/ROLE] will send the brief by [DATE] — send questions to [NAME/EMAIL].' The 'to get ready for kickoff' setup doesn't add anything and reads a bit like motivational padding.
